_meta.purpose: Hidden structures not explicit in the problem statement — recognize and model them
_meta.usage: Stage 2.5 and Stage 3.5 — before committing to approach, check if problem hides one of these
_meta.howToUse: Read recognize conditions for each. If 2+ conditions match your problem — that structure is likely present.

implicitStructures.name: Hidden DAG
implicitStructures.shortDescription: Dependencies flow one way — no cycles
recognize: Problem mentions prerequisites or dependencies
Some elements must be processed before others
There is a natural ordering constraint between elements
Problem asks if all tasks can be completed
Problem asks for ordering of elements satisfying constraints
howToModel: Create node per element
Add directed edge A→B if A must come before B
Check for cycles — if cycle exists, problem may be unsolvable
Apply topological sort to get valid ordering
Apply DP in topological order for optimization
templatesThatFollow: Topological Sort (Kahn's BFS or DFS post-order)
examples: Course prerequisites — can you take all courses?
Build order — in what order to compile files?
Task scheduling with dependencies
Alien dictionary — infer character ordering
implicitStructures.watchOut: If graph has cycles — standard DAG algorithms fail. Must detect and handle cycles first.

implicitStructures.name: Hidden Graph from Constraints
implicitStructures.shortDescription: Graph defined by a rule not explicit edge list
recognize: Problem says 'can reach from i to j if condition holds'
Elements are connected if they share a property
Transition between states is defined by an operation
Grid movement problems — cells are nodes, moves are edges
howToModel: Do NOT build explicit adjacency list if graph is too large
Generate neighbors on the fly during BFS/DFS
Define getNeighbors(node) function that computes adjacent nodes
Use visited set to avoid reprocessing
examples: Grid navigation — cells connected by movement rules
Word ladder — words connected if differ by one letter
Jumps in array — index i connects to i + a[i] and i - a[i]
Number transformation — x connects to x+1, x-1, 2x
implicitStructures.watchOut: Generating neighbors on the fly requires careful bounds checking. State space must be finite.

implicitStructures.name: Hidden Bipartite Structure
implicitStructures.shortDescription: Elements naturally split into two groups with edges only between groups
recognize: Problem involves assigning elements to one of two groups
Elements of same type never directly interact
Problem involves matching workers to jobs, students to schools
Conflict graph — two elements conflict means they cannot be in same group
Graph is 2-colorable
howToModel: Try 2-coloring the graph with BFS
If 2-colorable — bipartite confirmed
Left set and right set defined by coloring
Apply bipartite matching if needed
templatesThatFollow: Maximum Independent Set (= n - maximum matching for bipartite)
examples: Is it possible to divide people into two groups with no internal conflict?
Assign tasks to workers — each task needs specific worker type
Check if graph can be colored with 2 colors
implicitStructures.watchOut: Bipartite matching is O(V * E). For large graphs verify this fits in time budget.

implicitStructures.name: Hidden Tree from Parent Array
implicitStructures.shortDescription: Array where a[i] = parent of node i defines a tree
recognize: Array where each element points to another index
Problem says 'node i has parent a[i]'
Pointer structure — each node has exactly one outgoing pointer
Functional graph where each node has out-degree 1
howToModel: Build adjacency list from parent array
Root is the node with no parent or self-loop
Run DFS/BFS from root
Apply tree DP bottom-up
examples: Given parent array, find diameter of tree
Sum of distances to all other nodes
Find LCA of two nodes
implicitStructures.watchOut: If a[i] can point to i itself — that is the root. If multiple self-loops — it is a forest not a tree.

implicitStructures.name: Hidden Monotonic Stack Structure
implicitStructures.shortDescription: For each element, answer depends on nearest larger/smaller element
recognize: For each element, need to find nearest element greater than it
For each element, need to find nearest element smaller than it
Problem involves histograms, stock prices, buildings
Need previous or next element satisfying a comparison condition
howToModel: Maintain stack of indices
For next greater — use decreasing stack
For next smaller — use increasing stack
Pop when current element satisfies the condition relative to stack top
examples: Largest rectangle in histogram
Daily temperatures — days until warmer
Stock span problem
Trapping rain water
implicitStructures.watchOut: Monotonic stack gives O(n) where brute force is O(n^2). Always prefer it for next greater/smaller.

implicitStructures.name: Hidden Interval Structure from Events
implicitStructures.shortDescription: Start and end times define intervals — use sweep line
recognize: Elements have start and end times or positions
Problem asks about overlaps, coverage, or scheduling
Events happen at specific times — arrival and departure
Problem involves ranges on a number line
howToModel: Create event list — (time, type) pairs where type is start or end
Sort events by time
Sweep through events maintaining active count
Maximum active count = maximum overlap
examples: Minimum meeting rooms needed
Maximum number of overlapping intervals
Merge all overlapping intervals
Find if a person is free at time T
implicitStructures.watchOut: When two events have same timestamp — end events before start events to avoid counting overlap incorrectly.

implicitStructures.name: Hidden Prefix Sum Structure
implicitStructures.shortDescription: Subarray property reduces to difference of prefix values
recognize: Problem asks about sum of contiguous subarray
Looking for subarray with sum equal to target
Range sum queries on static array
Problem involves cumulative values
howToModel: Build prefix sum array: pre[i] = a[0] + a[1] + ... + a[i-1]
Sum of subarray [l, r] = pre[r+1] - pre[l]
For subarray sum = target: pre[r+1] - pre[l] = target → pre[l] = pre[r+1] - target
Use HashMap to find pre[l] in O(1)
templatesThatFollow: Prefix Sum array O(n) build O(1) query
Prefix Sum + HashMap (subarray sum equals target)
examples: Subarray sum equals k — count all such subarrays
Longest subarray with sum at most k
Range sum query on matrix
implicitStructures.watchOut: Prefix sum only works for sum queries. For min/max queries use Sparse Table or Segment Tree.

implicitStructures.name: Hidden DP on String Structure
implicitStructures.shortDescription: String problem where subproblems are prefixes or substrings
recognize: Problem involves optimal way to split or parse a string
Problem asks for minimum edits to transform one string to another
Looking for longest common subsequence or substring
Problem involves matching patterns in string
howToModel: For two strings — dp[i][j] typically involves prefixes s1[0..i] and s2[0..j]
For one string — dp[i] or dp[i][j] for prefix or substring
Transitions look at current character match or mismatch
Base cases are empty string or single character
examples: Edit distance between two strings
Longest common subsequence
Word break — can string be split into dictionary words
Regular expression matching
implicitStructures.watchOut: 2D DP on strings at n=m=10^4 needs 400MB. Check memory. Consider space optimization.

implicitStructures.name: Hidden Tree DP Structure
implicitStructures.shortDescription: Optimal answer for subtree computable from children's answers
recognize: Input is a tree (explicitly or as parent array)
Problem asks for property of each node based on its subtree
Optimal global answer requires combining subtree answers
Problem involves paths through the tree
howToModel: Root the tree at node 0 or given root
DFS post-order — process all children before current node
dp[node] = f(dp[child1], dp[child2], ...) for some function f
For path problems — track best path going up through each node
templatesThatFollow: Rerooting technique (for problems where root changes)
examples: Maximum path sum in binary tree
Diameter of tree
Minimum vertex cover of tree
Maximum independent set on tree
implicitStructures.watchOut: Recursive DFS on tree with n=10^5 may stack overflow. Use iterative DFS with explicit stack.

implicitStructures.name: Hidden Digit DP Structure
implicitStructures.shortDescription: Counting numbers in range satisfying a digit-level property
recognize: Count numbers from 1 to N satisfying some property
Property depends on individual digits not magnitude
N can be very large (up to 10^18)
Property involves digit sum, digit count, specific digit patterns
howToModel: Process number digit by digit from most significant
State: (position, tight_constraint, accumulated_property)
tight = true means digits so far equal to N's prefix — cannot exceed
tight = false means free to use any digit 0-9
Memoize on (position, tight, property_state)
examples: Count numbers up to N with digit sum equal to k
Count numbers with no two adjacent same digits
Count lucky numbers (only 4s and 7s) up to N
Count numbers divisible by k with specific digit constraints
implicitStructures.watchOut: tight constraint must be tracked carefully. Off-by-one on transitions causes wrong answers.

implicitStructures.name: Hidden Functional Graph (Rho structure)
implicitStructures.shortDescription: Each node has exactly one outgoing edge — forms rho (ρ) shape
recognize: Each element maps to exactly one other element
Array where a[i] defines next state from i
Iterating a function f(f(f(...f(x)...)))
Problem involves finding cycles in this structure
howToModel: Each node has out-degree exactly 1
Structure consists of several rho shapes — a tail leading into a cycle
Floyd's cycle detection or coloring to find cycles
Binary lifting for fast k-step queries
examples: Find cycle in sequence defined by next[i] = f(i)
Happy number problem — does iteration reach 1 or cycle?
Array permutation cycles
implicitStructures.watchOut: Do not confuse with general directed graph. Functional graph has special rho structure.

implicitStructures.name: Hidden Sliding Window Structure
implicitStructures.shortDescription: Optimal contiguous subarray where window validity is monotonic
recognize: Looking for optimal contiguous subarray
When you extend window it only gets worse in one direction
Shrinking from left can restore validity
Constraint on sum, count of distinct elements, or max-min difference
howToModel: Two pointers — left and right
Expand right pointer one step at a time
When window becomes invalid — shrink from left until valid again
Track optimal answer at each valid window state
templatesThatFollow: Sliding Window with Monotonic Deque (min/max in window)
examples: Longest substring without repeating characters
Minimum window containing all characters of pattern
Maximum sum subarray of size k
Longest subarray with at most k distinct elements
implicitStructures.watchOut: Sliding window ONLY works when window validity is monotonic. If adding element can make valid then invalid then valid again — sliding window breaks.

implicitStructures.name: Hidden Union Find Structure
implicitStructures.shortDescription: Dynamic grouping — merge groups, query same group
recognize: Elements are dynamically grouped as edges are added
Problem asks if two elements are in same connected component
Edges are added one by one — need connectivity after each
Problem involves merging sets or groups
howToModel: Initialize each element as its own component
For each edge (u, v) — union(u, v)
For query same component — find(u) == find(v)
Use path compression and union by rank for O(α(n)) per operation
examples: Number of connected components after adding edges
Detect cycle in undirected graph
Kruskal's minimum spanning tree
Friend circles — are A and B in same group?
implicitStructures.watchOut: Union Find handles undirected dynamic connectivity. For directed graphs use SCC algorithms.

detectionQuestions.question: Does any element depend on or constrain another element?
detectionQuestions.ifYes: Hidden DAG — model dependencies as directed edges

detectionQuestions.question: Can all elements be cleanly divided into two non-interacting groups?
Does each element point to exactly one other element as parent?
detectionQuestions.ifYes: Hidden Tree — reconstruct and apply tree DP

detectionQuestions.question: Do elements have start and end times or positions?
detectionQuestions.ifYes: Hidden Interval structure — use sweep line or sorting + greedy

detectionQuestions.question: Does the problem reduce to sum of a contiguous range?
detectionQuestions.ifYes: Hidden Prefix Sum — build prefix array

detectionQuestions.question: For each element, do you need to find the nearest element satisfying a comparison?
Is N up to 10^18 and property depends on individual digits?
detectionQuestions.ifYes: Hidden Digit DP — process digit by digit with tight constraint

detectionQuestions.question: Does each element map to exactly one other element?
detectionQuestions.ifYes: Hidden Functional Graph — rho structure with cycles

detectionQuestions.question: Are groups being merged dynamically as you process edges?
Looking for optimal contiguous subarray where validity shrinks monotonically?
